_comment: Source of truth for making page cards. Edit here to update. section: gravity | materials | constraints

cards.title: Bioluminescent
cards.description: A pool of paint dragged across a navy ground with a single pass. The pigment weight does the separating — heavier colors sink, lighter ones ride the surface. One luminous form emerged from the dark field and didn't move.

cards.title: The shower curtain problem
cards.description: The same swipe technique, completely different result. Tall format, teal and emerald over cobalt, silicone added to break the surface into cells. I wanted it to match my shower curtain. It does. That's a specific kind of design confidence.

cards.title: Leftover palette
cards.description: Leftover paint. Round canvas. The circular format contains the fluid differently — there are no corners to escape into, so the movement becomes orbital. The palette is constrained to one color family because that's what was left.

cards.title: Reef section
cards.description: Dutch pour uses a directed air stream to push pools of paint across the surface. The air blast separates colors and opens cells in a way gravity alone won't do. White ground, cobalt and teal and emerald and black. Like looking at a reef section under glass.

cards.title: The fold-down shelf
cards.description: A board on folding brackets, screwed to the wall next to my desk. When the bracket is flat against the wall, it's a painting. When it's open at 90 degrees, it's a shelf. One object, two states. I drilled countersunk holes before pouring so the surface would be uninterrupted.

cards.title: Three vases
cards.description: A paint skin is what happens when you pour onto a flat surface, let it dry completely, and peel it up. The skin preserves every movement from the original pour — cell structure, directional flow, color separations. Wrap that skin around a vase and it remembers where it came from.

cards.type: Resin and mica on a cup turner
cards.title: Centrifugal
cards.description: A cup turner spins the substrate continuously while resin and mica powder are applied. The centrifugal force pushes material outward and creates concentric swirl patterns — the design tool is rotation, not gravity. Different physics, different result.

cards.title: The whisky bottle
cards.description: The bottle is a portrait of what lives inside it. The incense produces smoke that falls rather than rises — it cascades down. I poured green paint while the bottle stood upright, letting gravity pull it downward the way the smoke moves. A black and silver skin depicts the smoke itself. The stopper is packing material. When it burns, the bottle shows you what happens next.

cards.type: Horizontal banding on a found form
cards.title: Geological
cards.description: A found vessel painted in horizontal bands — charcoal, silver, blue, olive, purple. The palette of what was left after other projects. The mica catches light differently at every angle. I didn't design the color story. The studio gave it to me.

cards.title: The lamp
cards.description: The base was wood. The shade was wicker and seagrass. Neither was wrong — they just weren't right. I stripped the seagrass off the wire frame and rebuilt the shade using oiled paper panels. The rainbow arc pattern backlit by the bulb behaves like stained glass. The iridescent base moves between burgundy, copper, magenta, and blue depending on the angle.

cards.title: Pax et Amor
cards.description: The box is packing material. The panels are oiled paper cut and assembled to behave like stained glass when backlit. The image was generated with AI — I wanted a specific portrait and used the tool that could produce it. Inside, a light strip on a timer. PAX is both the cat's name and the Latin word for peace. The fur trim at the bottom is real fur.

cards.title: The plant stand
cards.description: Four to five layers of corrugated cardboard, laminated and shaped into a three-legged plant stand. It supports my full body weight. The top surface is a fluid pour in black and silver. The piece looks like cast iron. It is cardboard. This is the first in a series.

cards.title: The Pax Box
cards.description: A side table and toy storage box designed to look like the cat who uses it. Paint-poured resin top in Pax's colors, felt collar band with silver studs, motion-activated interior light, cat-proof latches.

cards.title: Pax house
cards.description: Built entirely from found and reclaimed materials. The roof is removable. To weatherproof it, I ironed plastic bags together into a stiff laminated sheet and used it to form the roof shell — the heat fuses the layers and the result is surprisingly rigid. The floor is lined the same way. It is waterproof.
